posts.title: Falling
posts.excerpt: ...that there is a song behind every fall...

content.text: A feather
A leaf
Swing gently to the ground
To the same tune that carries the sails of a dreamy fisherman in the azure
To the same march that drums its way through wildbeests' stampedes
To the same hum that echoes in the hearts of both this space and this sea
A feather
A leaf
A tear
All fall to the same chime that I heard on that lonely night when we learned
That there is a song behind every fall

posts.title: Me
posts.excerpt: No phone, no agenda, just coffee and forty-five minutes of actually waking up.

content.text: Three weeks ago I decided to stop looking at my phone for the first forty-five minutes of the day. I want to report back.
What I expected
Honestly I expected to feel virtuous and calm and like a person who has their life together. That's the version of this experiment I had seen online.
What actually happened
The first few days were uncomfortable in a way I didn't anticipate. Not anxious exactly — more like bored. Which I think means the same thing.
By week two something shifted. I started noticing the morning had texture. The coffee tasted like something. I had thoughts that weren't reactions to other people's thoughts.
Week three
I'm not a convert. I don't think this makes me better. But I do think there's something to the idea that the first input of the day sets a tone. I'm still figuring out what tone I want to set.

posts.title: Pursuit of Magic
posts.excerpt: Some things I wish someone had said, and some things I wouldn't have heard anyway.

content.text: We all have felt the magic of childlike wonder.

At some point, even that one bearded professor who seems to be a little thin on the top was but a young boy, spending hours daydreaming on the fuzzy white trails of airplanes in the sky. And your grandmother was once a little girl who felt the purest of blisses skipping pebbles and hearing the crunch of fresh snow beneath her feet. It is a universal feeling in which astonishment and awe, curiosity and fascination all come together to create something that I cannot describe in any other way than as simply magical. We all say that such childhood memories feel just like yesterday, but one day you are 25, 40, or 59, uncertain of where that child in you ended and where you began.But once in a while - once in a truly azure moon, - that sense of wonder shows back up. Like a long-awaited crack in a writer's block, or a muse's kiss, the sense of pure, buoyant joy makes an unexpected visit just to remind you and I of all that is good in this world. Looking back at my college years, I can think of two distinct times when I was blessed with moments of such unadulterated joy.

The first time was in 2018, a year that I refer to as 'floral.' It was a summer job - 5 days a week, 9 through 13, which consisted of helping a plant taxonomy lab move out of its headquarters as the professor neared his retirement. Most of my work involved organizing old documents, getting rid of duplicates and such. To say the least, this part of the job was not particularly eye-opening. But on several occasions as I carried crates of books and papers in and out of the laboratory, I stole a few glimpses of the technicians' work which consisted of pressing and creating dried plant samples for the herbarium. Having grown up in a little Siberian town, I was no stranger to drying leaves and flowers; almost every book in our household had played its role as a plant press. After all, some of my favorite memories are those of walking through golden forests with Baba Jenya and searching for pretty leaves amongst the endless carpets of fall. Yet, the plants in this lab were different. They were a part of a greater plan - these were the chosen ones, for the care and precision with which each leaf and twig was positioned, contemplated upon, repositioned, dusted, repositioned once again, and then finally sewn onto the mounting sheets and catalogued rivaled that of the greatest masters of the Renaissance. And their story did not just end there – these plants were precious specimen whose shapes and forms would guide scientists across the world. To me, it was a perfect marriage of science and art.

So one can only imagine my delight when one day, the technician asked me to help her file specimens in the herbarium. The moment I opened one of its cabinets, I was greeted by a thick leafy aroma, similar to that of my childhood library. Here were samples that dated all the way back to the Korean War, humbly sharing racks with the clumsier work of some biology undegrads. One day as I was working I even decided to play some classical music for these plants to enjoy. I continued this ritual throughout my time working in the lab, learning to create samples of my own amongst a maze of filing cabinets. I doubt that anyone but me enjoyed these musical numbers, but I do know for certain that sometime then I discovered my love for creating and preserving objects of beauty.

My second happiest moment took place more recently. The setting was not exactly ideal - Coronovirus had sealed me in my 5 square metre apartment, finals were in full swing and I was working on the last, most challenging illustrations for professor'ss upcoming book. The book told the story of laboratories, starting with those of mysterious alchemists all the way to the massive institutions of today. Pencil shavings, eraser dust, sketches, books and papers were splayed all across my bed in which I had not slept for three nights. The tiny room reeked of stale coffee. I only had one more night to create the background of a certain drawing, and I still was not sure where to start. Normally the mere mention of such a scenario would fill me with anxiety; yet I distinctly remember sitting at that messy desk as the sun began to rise and feeling an overwhelming sense of happiness.

Next morning, as I dozed off on the bus on my way to the publisher, I found myself repeating the same two lines over and over in my mind – 'I am finally done' and 'I want more.' This was by no means because the work was easy. Illustrating the history of science required a great amount of research, after which there was the task of assembling that puzzle of facts and data into a story. I wanted this story to transcend any readers’ image of laboratories as cold and standoffish; instead, I wanted the readers to empathize with hardworking people at each laboratory’s heart and to hear the whirring of the centrifuge and the afternoon coffee chatter. It was on this bus ride that I realized just how much I loved drawing and creating stories, and that I would never, ever stop doing so. When the first copy of the book finally arrived, I could not begin to describe what it felt like to hold it in my hands, to know that a story I had helped bring to life was now part of something much larger than myself.
Nothing is more magical than childlike wonder, and in remembering the most profound, happiest moments of my life, I have found this magic in the crackle of blank paper, in the stories to be told
